coredata.dc:title: Construction of stable and multifunctional tellurium nanoparticles using chitosan and carbon quantum dots as functional templates

Huang, Chao
Ye, Xiguang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, chitosan (CS) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were chosen to be functional templates to synthesize tellurium nanoparticles (TeNPs) with high stability, antioxidant, and photoluminescence properties. The synergistic interaction between CS and CQDs not only ensured the stable dispersion of TeNPs, achieving a monodisperse state with precisely controlled particle sizes of 2.7 ± 0.5 nm and a stable zeta potential of 39.07 mV, but also demonstrated high stability under various environmental factors, including pH, salt ion concentrations, and temperature. Furthermore, this synergistic effect significantly enhanced the antioxidant efficacy of TeNPs, effectively scavenging DPPH, ABTS, and hydroxyl radicals. Concurrently, it endowed TeNPs with intense and stable photoluminescence properties, making them ideal for fluorescent probes, particularly exhibiting exceptional potential in the fluorescence detection of Hg2+ ions. The results of this study fully demonstrated the significant importance of CS and CQDs in enhancing performance and broadening functionality in the synthesis of nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Carbon quantum dots
Tellurium nanoparticles
Photoluminescence properties
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing detection of Hg2+ based on CS-CQDs-TeNPs
Optimization of fluorescence sensing detection parameters
Fluorescence sensing detection of Hg2+ in environmental water samples
Fluorescence detection Hg2+ based on CS-CQDs-TeNPs
Fluorescence detection Hg2+ in environmental water samples
The mechanism of fluorescence detection Hg2+ based on CS-CQDs-TeNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CS-CQDs-TeNPs. Distribution of DH (A). Zeta potential (B). TEM image at low magnification (inset is the size distribution histogram and high magnification image) (C). SEM image (D). Full-survey XPS spectra (E), C 1s peak of XPS spectra (F), O 1s peak of XPS spectra (G), Te 3d of XPS spectra (H), FTIR spectrum (I), XRD pattern (J).

Optical properties of CS-CQDs-TeNPs. UV–Vis absorption spectra (black line, Abs), excitation spectra (red dotted line, Ex), and emission spectra (blue line, Em) of CS-CQDs-TeNPs (A). Fluorescence emission spectra of CS-CQDs-TeNPs at the excitation wavelengths of 360 to 450 nm(B).
Antioxidant activities of CS, Na2TeO3, and CS-CQDs-TeNPs in scavenging ·DPPH (A), ABTS+·(B), and ·OH (C) free radicals.

DH and intensity (A for different pH, E for different NaCl concentrations, I for different heating temperatures), zeta potential and PDI (B for different pH, F for different NaCl concentrations, J for different heating temperatures), fluorescence intensity (C for different pH, G for different NaCl concentrations, K for different heating temperatures), and UV–Vis absorption spectra (D for different pH, H for different NaCl concentrations, L for different heating temperatures) of CS-CQDs-TeNPs at different conditions. Bars with different characters indicated a statistical difference (p < 0.05) among different conditions.

Fluorescence detection of Hg2+. Curve of the relative fluorescence intensity(I/I0) of CS-CQDs-TeNPs with addition of Hg2+ solution (0.39–100.00 μmol/L), and the inset was the fluorescence spectra of CS-CQDs-TeNPs corresponding to different concentrations of Hg2+ (A). The linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of CS-CQDs-TeNPs and Hg2+ concentration ranging from 1.56 μmol/L to 12.50 μmol/L (B). Selective test for Hg2+ detection. Histogram of the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of CS-CQDs-TeNPs quenched by different metal ions with the concentration of 100 μmol/L (C). Interferent test for Hg2+ detection. Histogram of the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of CS-CQDs-TeNPs quenched by Hg2+ (100 μmol/L) and other interferents (100 μmol/L) (D).
Characterization of CS-CQDs-TeNPs interacted with Hg2+. FTIR spectra (A). Full-survey XPS spectra (B), C 1s spectra (C), O 1s spectra (D), Te 3d spectra (E), Hg 4f spectra (F).
The UV–Vis absorption spectra (A), fluorescence lifetime curves (B), and zeta potential (C) of CS-CQDs-TeNPs with and without the presence of 100 μmol/L Hg2+.
The fabrication procedure of CS-CQDs-TeNPs (A), the identification of CS-CQDs-TeNPs (B), and the development of a fluorescence sensor based on CS-CQDs-TeNPs for Hg2+ detection (C).
Fluorescence detection Hg2+ in environmental water samples based on the CS-CQDs-TeNPs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Construction of stable and multifunctional tellurium nanoparticles using chitosan and carbon quantum dots as functional templates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Zhongshan 528458, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Zhongshan 528458, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Changmingshui Avenue, Wuguishan Street, Zhongshan 528458, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, chitosan (CS) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were chosen to be functional templates to synthesize tellurium nanoparticles (TeNPs) with high stability, antioxidant, and photoluminescence properties. The synergistic interaction between CS and CQDs not only ensured the stable dispersion of TeNPs, achieving a monodisperse state with precisely controlled particle sizes of 2.7 ± 0.5 nm and a stable zeta potential of 39.07 mV, but also demonstrated high stability under various environmental factors, including pH, salt ion concentrations, and temperature. Furthermore, this synergistic effect significantly enhanced the antioxidant efficacy of TeNPs, effectively scavenging DPPH, ABTS, and hydroxyl radicals. Concurrently, it endowed TeNPs with intense and stable photoluminescence properties, making them ideal for fluorescent probes, particularly exhibiting exceptional potential in the fluorescence detection of Hg2+ ions. The results of this study fully demonstrated the significant importance of CS and CQDs in enhancing performance and broadening functionality in the synthesis of nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan (CS), derived from the alkaline deacetylation of chitin, was a cationic basic polymer that boasted exceptional biocompatibility and a multitude of functional attributes, encompassing antibacterial, antioxidant, adsorptive, and chelating capabilities. These qualities rendered it highly versatile across various domains, including biomedicine, food science, and the chemical industry [1].The molecular structure of CS was abundant in hydroxyl and amino functional groups, while its polymer chain exhibited remarkable flexibility [2]. These distinctive structures not only promoted effective interactions with nanomaterials, ensuring their stable dispersion and functional enhancement, but also equipped CS with the proficiency to efficiently adsorb and chelate metal ions. In general, CS played a pivotal role in stabilizing and functionalizing nanomaterials, as well as in enriching, adsorbing, and removing metal ions [3].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a size below 10 nm, exhibited excellent photoluminescence properties and were distinguished by their ease of synthesis and functionalization, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [4]. Currently, CQDs were primarily used as fluorescent probes and had found widespread applications in the fields of chemical sensing, bioimaging, medicine and other fields [5]. In recent years, surface modification strategies, such as surface passivation and heteroatom doping, had significantly enhanced the luminescence properties of CQDs, and introduced active sites (such as hydrophilic groups such as -OH and -COOH and CO, CC) on their surfaces that enable the interactions with other substances. These active sites not only promoted the sensing and catalytic process of CQDs, but also strengthened their ability to stably disperse nanomaterials, thereby further expanding the range of application for CQDs [6,7]. In summary, both CS and CQDs exhibit great potential and value in the stable dispersion and functionalization of nanomaterials, as well as in the removal and detection of metal ions.

Tellurium (Te), a metalloid, shares membership in the chalcogen group with selenium (Se). In recent years, It had garnered significant attention owing to its optical, electrical, thermal, and biomedical properties that mirror those of selenium [8]. In nature, Te existed in various forms, including elemental tellurium, inorganic tellurium, and organic tellurium. Notably, tellurium nanoparticles (TeNPs) exhibited superior performance in optoelectronic properties and also possessed remarkable antioxidant and anticancer biological activities. These characteristics made it have broad application prospects in the fields of optoelectronics and biomedicine, similar to those of nano‑selenium [9]. However, the poor water solubility and stability of TeNPs cause it to easily aggregated and precipitated, thereby reducing its functional properties, particularly its biological functionalities, and limiting the scope of its practical applications [10]. Hence, construction of highly stable and active TeNPs had become a pivotal direction for advanced research and optimization within the field of Te [11]. Among the numerous preparation methods for TeNPs, the soft template method had emerged as the preferred method due to its mild reaction conditions and straightforward operation procedures [12]. Notably, the utilization of soft templates possessing specific functional properties could not only effectively stabilize and disperse TeNPs but also further enhance or endow new functional characteristics to it. Therefore, exploring suitable functional soft template was of great significance for enhancing the performance of TeNPs and expanding its application fields.

Aiming to construct stable and multifunctional TeNPs, this study utilized CS and β-cyclodextrin structure-like nitrogen-doped CQDs (β-CD-CQDs) as functional soft templates to synergistically stabilize, disperse, and functionalize the TeNPs, ultimately resulting in the formation of chitosan‑carbon quantum dots‑tellurium nanoparticles (CS-CQDs-TeNPs) with strong photoluminescence and antioxidant properties. Furthermore, a systematic investigation was conducted into their basic characteristics, functional properties, and stability. Additionally, based on the high affinity of chalcogens for Hg2+, CS-CQDs-TeNPs were utilized as specific fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection to construct a fluorescent sensor, which was explored its potential application in fluorescence rapid detection of Hg2+. Through the related results in this study, the intention was to provide a scientific theoretical foundation for expanding the research and application scope of TeNPs, CS, and CQDs, and to offer novel approaches for the rapid quantitative detection of Hg2+.

Chitosan (chemical formula: (C6H11NO4)n, deacetylation degree ≥95 %, molecular weight: 1526.45, a viscosity polymer (50–500 mPa·s) containing hydroxyl and amino groups (Fig. 1I)), Vitamin C (Vc), Citric acid (CA), and HgCl2 were purchased from Beijing Newprobe Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), NaAcˑ3H2O, NaHCO3, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, MnSO4ˑH2O, CaCl2, FeCl3ˑ6H2O, ZnCl2, AlCl3ˑ6H2O, Cr(NO3)3ˑ9H2O, NiCl2ˑ6H2O, PbCl2, CdCl2ˑ5/2H2O, and CoCl2ˑ6H2O were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CuSO4, SrCl2ˑ6H2O, and HAc were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). MgCl2ˑ6H2O and KBr were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China, Shanghai). HCl, NaOH, H2O2, and NaNO3 were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). All other reagents were analytically pure. All aqueous solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩˑcm) from FBZ2001-UP-P water purifier (Qingdao Fulham Technology Co., Ltd., China).

The kits used were as follows: The DPPH free radical scavenging rate detection kit (microplate method), Total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC) assay kit (ABTS microplate method), and Hydroxyl radical scavenging rate detection kit (Fenton microplate method) were purchased from leagene Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China, Shanghai).

1 g each of β-CD, urea, and CA were dissolved in 10 mL of Milli-Q water and reacted to microwave (800 W) for 5 min. The mixtures were then resuspended in an equal volume of Milli-Q water. Following this, centrifugation was performed at 6000 r/min under 25 °C for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone membrane and lyophilized.

A solution containing β-CD-CQDs (2.5 g/L), CS (0.4 %), and Na2TeO3 (100 mmol/L) was mixed and incubated for 15 min. Subsequently, a Vc solution (500 mmol/L) was added in a specified molar ratio and mixed via magnetic stirring for 1 min. The mixtures underwent reaction in a water bath for varying durations and were then dialyzed (using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 8000 Da) against distilled water for 24 h to obtain purified CS-CQDs-TeNPs.

Based on the aforementioned preparation method, the effects of the concentrations of Vc and Na2TeO3 at 6:1–24:4 (mmol: mmol), the dosages of β-CD-CQDs from 0 to 1500 mg/L, the ratios of Vc to Na2TeO3 at 2:1–12:1 (mmol: mmol), the reaction time from 0.25 to 6.00 h, and the reaction temperatures at 20–100 °C on the fabrication of CS-CQDs-TeNPs was investigated.

The hydrodynamic diameter (DH), total intensity, polydispersity index (PDI), and zeta potential of CS-CQDs-TeNPs were determined by a Zetasizer Nano ZS 90 (Malvern, UK). Subsequently, the JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) and the JSM-7610FPlus scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, Japan) were used to observe the morphology of CS-CQDs-TeNPs. Additionally, the elemental composition and surface properties analysis were conducted on the Vertex 70 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific, U.S.A.) and the XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, U.S.A.). The X-ray diffraction XRD patterns were measured on the X-ray polycrystalline powder diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs were measured by a transient absorption spectroscopy (TA FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK).

In the characterization of optical properties, the fluorescence spectra were recorded on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). and the UV–Vis spectra were analyzed on the UV-1800 dual-beam spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). For antioxidant properties, the DPPH, ABTS, and hydroxyl radicals scavenging rates were measured by the appropriate kits.

The stability of CS-CQDs-TeNPs was evaluated through the measurement of its colloidal chemical properties (such as DH, Count Rate, PDI, and Zeta potential) and optical properties(including UV–Vis spectra) after being dispersed and reacted in solutions with varying pH levels(ranging from 2 to 9), and NaCl/Na2SO4 concentrations (0 to 500 mmol/L) for 30 min. Additionally, the thermal stability of CS-CQDs-TeNPs was assessed by heating it in a water bath at temperatures ranging from 20 °C to 100 °C for duration of 30 to 60 min. Specifically, the mixtures were diluted 10-fold for colloidal chemical property measurements and 50-fold for optical property measurements, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing detection of Hg2+ based on CS-CQDs-TeNPs

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence stability of CS-CQDs-TeNPs was evaluated by measuring its fluorescence spectra (slit width of 10 nm, excitation wavelength of 370 nm with high sensitivity) after reaction for 30 min under various conditions, such as different pH levels (ranging from 2 to 12), NaCl concentrations (0 to 2 mol/L), anion species (NaCl, NaHCO3, NaAc, Na2CO3, Na2SO3, and Na2SO4 at 100 μmol/L and 1000 μmol/L), and H2O2 concentrations (0 to 1 mol/L). Specifically, 0.1 mL of CS-CQDs-TeNPs solution was dispersed in a variety of NaAc buffer solution, NaCl solution, anion solution, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of fluorescence sensing detection parameters
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment of the fluorescence quenching effect, CS-CQDs-TeNPs (diluted to 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 times) were dispersed in NaAc buffer solution with a pH of 5. Subsequently, varying concentrations of Hg2+ solutions (1, 10, and 100 μmol/L) were added to react for a duration ranging from 5 to 40 min. Following the reaction, the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were measured.

Fluorescence intensity and UV–Vis absorption were recorded as the optical response signals for detecting Hg2+. 100 μL of CS-CQDs-TeNPs solution mixed with 4.775 mL of NaAc buffer solution (0.2 mol/L, pH 5) was reacted with various concentrations of Hg2+ (125 μL, ranging from 0.39 to 100.00 μmol/L) for 10 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the mixtures were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (parameter settings consistent with those in Section 2.5.1).

To evaluate the selectivity and the interference tolerance of the sensing system relying on the aforementioned fluorescence assay, the fluorescence intensity of various heavy metal ions (Mn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Cr3+) at equal concentrations were tested in the presence and absence of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing detection of Hg2+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze the detection performance of the aforementioned fluorescence sensing method for Hg2+, we selected the cestbon purified water purchased from the local supermarket, tap water from the laboratory faucet, and lake water from the school's artificial lake as the typical environmental water samples, and used the spiking method to test the environmental water samples by adding 3, 6, and 12 μmol/L of Hg2+, respectively. The tests were performed in triplicate and the results were averaged.

Data from at least three independent experiments were reported as the mean ± standard deviation. The statistical significance of differences among groups was assessed using Fisher's Least Significant Difference (LSD) test, implemented through SAS software (Windows version 9.2). Significant variations (p < 0.05) were distinguished by unique lowercase letters.

Scheme 1 illustrated the preparation, characterization, and application of CS-CQDs-TeNPs. Here, CS and CQDs, abundant in hydrophilic groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino, served as soft templates to synergistically stabilize and modify TeNPs[13]. The interaction between CS and Te facilitated efficient dispersion of TeNPs and enhanced its antioxidant properties. Concurrently, CQDs, functioning as a template binding with Te, imparted remarkable fluorescence characteristics to the TeNPs, ultimately yielding multifunctional CS-CQDs-TeNPs.

The stability and functional attributes of CS-CQDs-TeNPs could be modulated by adjusting reaction parameters in the soft template approach [14]. Consequently, a series of single-factor experiments were conducted, encompassing reactant concentrations and ratios, template dosages, reaction time, and temperature, to produce a multitude of CS-CQDs-TeNPs featuring small particle size, robust stability, and intense fluorescence intensity. Specifically, employing a Vc and Na2TeO3 ratio of 24 mmol/L: 3 mmol/L, a CS concentration of 500 mg/L, CQDs of 1250 mg/L, a reaction time of 0.5 h, and a temperature of 60 °C (Supplementary Figs. S1-S5), resulted in the synthesis of CS-CQDs-TeNPs with a DH of 98.41 nm and a positively charged zeta potential of 39.07 mV. These nanoparticles exhibited concentrated distribution, with a PDI of 0.59 and a total scattering intensity of 155.80 kcps (Fig. 1A-B). In brief, CS acting as soft template, could efficiently interact with Te to achieve a high dispersion of CS-CQDs-TeNPs and adsorbing onto surfaces to impart it with positive charge.

Moreover, the surface morphologies of CS-CQDs-TeNPs were examined using TEM and SEM. As shown in Fig. 1C-D, the monodisperse CS-CQDs-TeNPs exhibited a spherical shape with a quantum size approximately 2.7 ± 0.5 nm (n = 95), as determined by Gaussian fitting. This finding aligned with physicochemical analyses and reaffirmed the high stability and dispersity of the CS-CQDs-TeNPs.

The XPS was further analyzed the elemental composition and surface states of CS-CQDs-TeNPs. Fig. 1E presented the full-survey spectrum of CS-CQDs-TeNPs, revealing photoelectron peaks corresponding to Te 3d, O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, with the atomic percentages 0.16 %, 34.8 %, 6.12 %, and 58.9 %, repectively. This result confirmed the presence of these elements on the surface of CS-CQDs-TeNPs and highlighted the role of CS and CQDs as effective soft templates for TeNPs encapsulation and stabilization. High-resolution analysis of C 1s, O 1s, and Te 3d was conducted (Fig. 1F-H). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) exhibited three prominent peaks at 283.25, 284.80, and 286.50 eV, attributed to CH, C=C/C-C, and C-OH groups, respectively [15–17]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) featured two strong peaks at 529.53 and 531.12 eV, corresponding to O=C-N and O=C-C groups, respectively [18]. The Te 3d spectrum (Fig. 1H) showed binding energies at 573.51 and 583.48 eV, the binding energy of Te0 [19]. In summary, these findings validated the successful synthesis of TeNPs and its decoration with CS and CQDs, yielding CS-CQDs-TeNPs with abundant surface functional groups and active sites.

Furthermore, to explore the integration of CS, CQDs and TeNPs, FT-IR spectra were used to analyze the surface functional groups and chemical structures of these samples. As depicted in Fig. 1I, CS, CQDs and CS-CQDs-TeNPs exhibited remarkably similar spectra, reaffirming the interaction between CS and CQDs with Te and their decoration on the surface of Te. The samples displayed characteristic absorption peaks at 3441.45 and 1629.4 cm−1, attributed to the O-H/N-H and NH bending vibrations [20,21]. Additionally, characteristic absorption peaks at 2921.20 and 1377.70 cm−1 likely represented CH stretching and bending vibrations [20,22,23], while the peak at 1025.50 cm−1 was linked to C-O-C stretching vibrations [24]. These observations indicated that CS and CQDs primarily interacted with Te through non-covalent bonds involving hydroxyl, amino, and ether groups. Notably, a new characteristic absorption peaks emerged at 589.20 cm−1, indicative of the TeO bond, suggesting covalent binding between the oxygen-containing functional groups of CS and CQDs with Te [25]. In summary, CS and CQDs stabilized and dispersed Te through both non-covalent and covalent interactions. In further analysis, the crystal structure of CS-CQDs-TeNPs was determined through XRD. As illustrated in Fig. 1J, the characteristic peaks of CS-CQDs-TeNPs aligned with the (101), (102), and (110) crystalline planes of Te (PDF#36-1452), confirming the effectiveness of CS and CQDs as functional soft templates in synthesizing Te. Additionally, a prominent broad peak centered at approximately 23° was observed, attributable to the amorphous carbon component of CS and CQDs [26,27].

In conclusion, the XPS, FTIR, and XRD results were in consensus, collectively confirming that CS and CQDs served as soft templates for effectively stabilizing and modifying TeNPs, ensuring that CS-CQDs-TeNPs possessed abundant surface sites and good hydrophilicity.

CQDs, as an novel soft template, could modify and impart TeNPs with exceptional fluorescence intensity. To comprehensively examine the optical properties of CS-CQDs-TeNPs, UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were conducted (Fig. 2 ). As illustrated in Fig. 2A, CS-CQDs-TeNPs had the UV–Vis absorption band in the range of 200–600 nm, with a distinctive peak at 335 nm, potentially indicative of the n-π* electronic transition absorption of typical CO groups within the templates [28]. Additionally, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CS-CQDs-TeNPs exhibited mirror symmetry, with an optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm and a corresponding maximum emission wavelength of 460 nm. The quantum yield measured by using quinine bisulfate as a standard was 17.95 %. The higher quantum yield was primarily attributed to the effective stabilization and functional modification of TeNPs by soft-template CQDs [29,30].

As depicted in Fig. 2B, CS-CQDs-TeNPs demonstrated excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, where the maximum emission wavelength varied from 453 nm to 531 nm with excitation ranging from 370 nm to 460 nm. This phenomenon could be attributed to variations in the size distribution of CS-CQDs-TeNPs and surface defects inherent to CQDs [31]. Based on these findings, CS-CQDs-TeNPs demonstrated peak fluorescence intensity when excited at 370 nm, prompting its selection for subsequent detailed investigations.

Nowadays, both TeNPs and CS were reported to have excellent antioxidant properties. In this study, the antioxidant activity of CS-CQDs-TeNPs capped by CS and CQDs was analyzed through radical scavenging rate assessments. As depicted in Fig. 3 , CS-CQDs-TeNPs exhibited high scavenging activity for ·DPPH, ABTS+·, and ·OH radicals, with scavenging activity directly proportional to the concentration of CS-CQDs-TeNPs. Specifically, the IC50 values for scavenging ·DPPH and ABTS+· radicals were determined to be 57.56 ± 1.92 and 88.98 ± 2.70 μg/mL, respectively, closely mirroring those of ascorbic acid (with an IC50 of 55.95 μg/mL [32]), thereby highlighting its potent antioxidant properties. In contrast, CS and Na2TeO3 exhibited weaker radical scavenging activity, further confirming that CS and CQDs serving as functionalized soft templates could effectively enhance the antioxidant properties of TeNPs, laying a solid foundation for its widespread applications in the biomedical field. It was remarkable that the CS-CQDs-TeNPs exhibited a scavenging ability for radicals in order of ·DPPH > ABTS+· > ·OH. This hierarchy might be attributed to the varying degree of electron deficiency among these three types of free radicals, which in turn affected their covalent structure and oxidation. Furthermore, the molecular weight of free radicals and the ease of their movement in solution also significantly influenced their reaction with TeNPs, leading to varied scavenging efficiencies [33].

The stability of CS-CQDs-TeNPs in aqueous solution is a crucial factor for its biological activity and applications in various fields [34]. In this study, the potential effects of pH, NaCl salt ions, and heating temperature on the colloidal chemical and optical stability of CS-CQDs-TeNPs, including its DH, derived count rate, zeta potential, fluorescence, and UV–Vis absorption, were investigated to assess the stability of CS-CQDs-TeNPs. An increase in DH indicated aggregation of nanoparticles. The derived count rate of the system was proportional to the sixth power of the particle diameter and the number of particles. PDI (polydispersity index) was closely related to the particle size distribution of nanoparticles, and the smaller PDI indicated the more concentrated the particle size distribution. Zeta potential indicated the electrostatic stability of nanoparticles, and the higher the absolute value of Zeta potential, the higher the electrostatic stability.

As could be seen form Fig. 4A-C, compared to the blank group, within the pH range of 2 to 4, the CS-CQDs-TeNPs particles did not aggregate, exhibiting high electrostatic stability, but its fluorescence performance was weakened. As the pH increased from 5 to 8, the CS-CQDs-TeNPs remained non-aggregation and demonstrated strong fluorescence performance, albeit with reduced electrostatic stability. Further, when the pH was raised to 9, severe aggregation of CS-CQDs-TeNPs particles was observed, causing decreased stability, whereas their fluorescence properties remained unaffected. As shown in Fig. 4D, there were no significant change in the UV–visible absorption spectra of CS-CQDs-TeNPs, indicating that its surface structural properties remain unchanged under different pH conditions. CS, a natural cationic polymer, exhibits high stability at the same pH due to electrostatic repulsion [35]. As for the photoluminescence properties of CQDs, excessively acidic conditions could weaken their fluorescence, while alkaline conditions could strengthen it [36]. In summary, under different pH conditions, the colloidal chemical properties of CS-CQDs-TeNPs were regulated by the template CS, exhibiting high stability within the pH range of 2 to 8. Meanwhile, its photoluminescent properties were modulated by the template CQDs, displaying high stability within the pH range of 5 to 9.

As shown in Fig. 4E-H, with the addition of NaCl ranging from 0.05 to 1.00 mol/L, the CS-CQDs-TeNPs did not aggregate. Its DH decreased and the particle size distribution remained concentrated. Additionally, the fluorescence properties and the surface structural properties were unaffected. However, the electrostatic stability decreased, which was presumed to be the electrostatic shielding effect of oppositely charged Cl− towards CS-CQDs-TeNPs [37], thereby reducing its electrostatic stability. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 4I-L, the effect of heat treatment on the stability of CS-CQDs-TeNPs was examined. As the heating temperature increased within the range of 40 °C to 100 °C, the DH of CS-CQDs-TeNPs decreased, suggesting that the intensified thermal motion of molecules at higher temperatures enhanced the dispersion of CS-CQDs-TeNPs particles (S. [38]). Notably, CS-CQDs-TeNPs maintained high electrostatic stability and robust fluorescence properties across various heating temperatures, with no significant alterations in its surface structural characteristics. Overall, CS-CQDs-TeNPs exhibited remarkable stability across a wide range of conditions, including a pH of 2 to 8, NaCl solutions ranging from 0.05 to 1.00 mol/L, and heating temperatures from 40 °C to 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection Hg2+ based on CS-CQDs-TeNPs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs endowed CS-CQDs-TeNPs with excellent fluorescence properties, as well as good fluorescence stability (Figs. 2, 4, and S6), rendering it potential candidates for use as fluorescent probe. Additionally, chalcogens exhibit a high affinity for Hg2+ [39]. Consequently, this study investigated the capability of CS-CQDs-TeNPs for the fluorescence detection of Hg2+.

As depicted in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CQDs-TeNPs decreased with the increasing concentration of Hg2+. A good linear relationship was observed between the I/I0 and the Hg2+ concentration within the range of 1.56 to 12.50 μmol/L, with a linear regression equation of I/I0 = -0.030c (Hg 2+ ) + 1.0268 (R2 = 0.9756) (Fig. 5B). Using the methodology outlined by Gan et al. [40], the limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ was found to be 0.29 μmol/L (where I was I0 + 3σ, and σ was the standard deviation obtained from 15 measurements of the blank solution, which was 1.51.).

On this basis, 100 μmol/L of Hg2+ and the most common coexisting interfering metal ions (Mn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Cr3+) were individually introduced to CS-CQDs-TeNPs to evaluate the selectivity of its fluorescence detection towards Hg2+ (Fig. 5C). The results showed that none of the coexisting metal ions significantly altered the fluorescence of CS-CQDs-TeNPs, with intensities varying between 82.98 % and 111.30 % of the blank group. In contrast, Hg2+ effectively quenched the fluorescence, reducing it to 35.73 % of the blank group. This confirmed the high selectivity of CS-CQDs-TeNPs for Hg2+ among the tested metal ions. Additionally, to assess the influence of coexisting metal ions on Hg2+ induced quenching of CQDs fluorescence, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CQDs-TeNPs after adding both Hg2+ and the coexisting metal ions (100 μmol/L each) was measured. The results indicated that the coexisting metal ions did not significantly interfere with the fluorescence sensing of CQDs towards Hg2+, with intensities ranging from 33.81 % to 57.26 % of the blank group (Fig. 5D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection Hg2+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of fluorescence detection of Hg2+ using CS-CQDs-TeNPs, experiments were conducted on the environmental water samples, including pure, tap, and lake water. Initially, none of these samples contained detectable levels of Hg2+ (Table 1 ). Subsequently, the standard addition method was employed to determine Hg2+ concentrations in these samples. As depicted in Table 1, the recovery ranged from 98.14 % to 107.31 % for pure water, 100.30 % to 108.24 % for tap water, and 100.74 % to 106.22 % for lake water, with corresponding relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 0.06 % to 4.22 %, 0.09 % to 4.19 %, and 1.09 % to 3.30 %, respectively. These results demonstrated the high reliability of the CS-CQDs-TeNPs-based fluorescence signal for detecting Hg2+ within a specific concentration range, underscoring its potential for practical application in environmental water analysis. However, compared to other fluorescence detection systems (Table S1), The fluorescence sensor of CS-CQDs-TeNPs had a narrower detection range and a higher LOD, limiting its application. Thus, further optimization and enhancement were required in future studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of fluorescence detection Hg2+ based on CS-CQDs-TeNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The aforementioned experiments demonstrated that Hg2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of CS-CQDs-TeNPs. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is closely related to the composition and surface state of the interaction between CS-CQDs-TeNPs and Hg2+ [41]. Therefore, the elemental composition and surface functional groups of the interaction between CS-CQDs-TeNPs and Hg2+ were analyzed using FT-IR and XPS.

Comparison of the FTIR spectra of CS-CQDs-TeNPs before and after the addition of Hg2+ (as shown in Fig. 6A), revealed blue shifts at the peaks at 2921.20 and 1377.70 cm−1, suggesting that the addition of Hg2+ could weaken the intramolecular hydrogen bonding of CH groups on the surface of CS-CQDs-TeNPs and adsorb onto its surface through intermolecular hydrogen bonding [42]. Furthermore, the significant shifts were observed at 3441.45, 1629.4, and 589.2 cm−1, corresponding to the O-H/N-H and TeO groups on the CS-CQDs-TeNPs surface. These shifts were likely related to the interaction between Hg2+ and O-/N-/Te- groups, resulting in alterations in their vibrational frequencies ([42]; C. [43]). The results indicated that Hg2+ interacted with the O/N-containing functional groups or Te atoms on the surface of CS-CQDs-TeNPs to quench its fluorescence.

Regarding the XPS results, the binding energy peak observed at 103.70 eV in the full-spectrum scan (Fig. 6B) was designated as the characteristic signal of Hg 4f, indicating the successful capture of Hg2+ by CS-CQDs-TeNPs. In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 6C), only two peaks appeared at 284.80 eV and 288.56 eV. Comparing with Fig. 1F, the binding energy of CH (283.25 eV) disappeared, and the binding energy of C-OH (286.50 eV) shifted towards the higher energy region, suggesting a strong chelating interaction between CH and OH groups on the surface of CS-CQDs-TeNPs with Hg2+ [44,45]. Additionally, the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s in Fig. 6D displayed the binding energies at 531.44 eV and 535.65 eV, which corresponded to the shifted binding energies of O=C-N and O=C-C groups towards the higher energy region, respectively. This indicated that the O-/N-containing groups on the surface of CS-CQDs-TeNPs were also sites for chelating with Hg2+ [45]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 6E, three distinct peaks were observed in the Te 3d spectrum. The peaks at 573.36 eV and 583.24 eV could be assigned to Te0, while the newly emerging binding energy at 564.36 eV (comparing with Fig. 1H), was presumed to be the value for HgTe formed after Te0 captures Hg2+. Meanwhile, Fig. 6F exhibited a binding energy peak at 100.36 eV, which aligned well with the values of Hg 4f in both Hg2+ and HgTe [46,47].

Furthermore, the quenching type of CS-CQDs-TeNPs was assessed via UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetime, and zeta potential measurements before and after Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 7A, the UV–Vis spectra of CS-CQDs-TeNPs after Hg2+ addition displayed a distinct redshift and significant peak intensity reduction, indicating the formation of larger complexes due to the binding of Hg2+ with CS-CQDs-TeNPs(S. [48]). The fluorescence lifetime results (Fig. 7B) revealed that the two fluorescence decay curves nearly overlapped, with an average lifetime of 9.24 ns (χ2 = 1.0255) for CS-CQDs-TeNPs and 9.02 ns (χ2 = 0.9264) after Hg2+ addition.This change was so small that it is negligible[49]. Additionally, zeta potential analysis (Fig. 7C) revealed a marked increase in surface charge of CS-CQDs-TeNPs after Hg2+ addition, indicating that Hg2+ binding enhanced the positive charge density [50]. Overall, these results confirmed that the quenching type of CS-CQDs-TeNPs follows static quenching.

In summary, the abundant O-/N-/C-functional groups of CS and the presence of Te in CS-CQDs-TeNPs synergistically enhance Hg2+ capture, promoting chelation with CQDs and effectively quenching its fluorescence via a static quenching mechanism.

In this study, CS and CQDs were successfully employed as functional templates to synthesize CS-CQDs-TeNPs with high stability, antioxidant activity, and photoluminescence properties. The synergistic interaction between CS and CQDs not only enabled the monodispersion of CS-CQDs-TeNPs with a controlled particle size of 2.7 ± 0.5 nm and a stable zeta potential of 39.07 mV, but also ensured excellent stability under various environmental conditions, including pH (2–8), salt ion concentrations (0.1–1 mol/L), and temperature (20–100 °C). Moreover, the CS-CQDs-TeNPs exhibited remarkable antioxidant capabilities, efficiently scavenging DPPH, ABTS, and hydroxyl radicals. Additionally, the CS-CQDs-TeNPs demonstrated strong and stable photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 17.95 %, making it ideal candidates for fluorescent probes. Notably, it showed exceptional potential for Hg2+ detection, as the fluorescence of CS-CQDs-TeNPs was effectively static quenched through strong chelation with Hg2+. This study highlights the significant role of CS and CQDs in enhancing the performance and expanding the functionality of nanomaterials, providing new insights for the development of multifunctional nanomaterials.

Xianying Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Haiyu Feng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Huijun Xian: Investigation. Xiaoyun Zheng: Investigation. Xiaorong Lin: Supervision, Resources. Weilin Zhou: Investigation. Yong Hu: Supervision, Resources. Yun Chen: Supervision, Resources. Chao Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xiguang Ye: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could appear to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Medical Science and Technology Research Fund Program of Guangdong [grant number A2023499] and the Undergraduate Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Guangdong Pharmaceutical University [grant number G202410573033].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.144101.
